lessons.title: CRO Foundation: Funnel Mapping and Conversion Research
lessons.title_es: Fundamentos de CRO: mapeo de embudo e investigación de conversión
lessons.description: Diagnose exactly where your funnel loses visitors before touching a single design element.
lessons.description_es: Diagnostica exactamente dónde tu embudo pierde visitantes antes de tocar un solo elemento de diseño.
lessons.content:
CRO Is a Research Discipline First

Conversion Rate Optimization is not

It is a structured research practice: understand why users aren't converting, form hypotheses grounded in evidence, test the hypotheses, and implement what works. Teams that run CRO as pure intuition rarely sustain improvements. Teams that run it as a research discipline compound gains quarter over quarter.

The Full-Funnel Audit

Before any test, map your conversion funnel end-to-end and instrument every step. For a service business, the funnel typically looks like: Ad impression → Landing page → Lead form → Thank-you page → Email sequence → Discovery call → Proposal → Closed deal. For ecommerce: Ad → Product page → Add to cart → Checkout → Purchase confirmation.

At each step, calculate the step conversion rate (users who complete the step ÷ users who reach it). Your lowest-converting step is your highest-leverage optimization target — this is not always what you'd guess. Many teams obsess over the CTA button while a 40% cart abandonment rate goes unaddressed.

Quantitative Research Tools

  • GA4: Funnel visualization, drop-off by step, segmentation by traffic source and device. Set up a custom funnel for your specific conversion path.
  • Hotjar / Microsoft Clarity: Heatmaps (where users click and don't click), scroll maps (where users stop reading), session recordings (watch real users navigate). Free tier covers most SMB needs.
  • Core Web Vitals: LCP, FID, CLS. Google's PageSpeed Insights. Performance problems kill conversions — a 1-second improvement in LCP increases conversions 3–7% on mobile.

Qualitative Research Methods

On-site surveys: Hotjar's exit-intent survey with one question:

Run for 2 weeks, collect 50+ responses. The top 3 objections are your highest-priority A/B test hypotheses.

Customer interviews: Talk to 5 people who converted and 5 who didn't. Ask: What almost stopped you? What made you decide to proceed? What did you wish you'd known earlier? Five interviews surface more actionable insight than any quantitative tool.

Competitor analysis: Use your CRO research to score competitor landing pages. Where are they building trust? What objections are they pre-empting? Copy what's working, improve what's missing.

lessons.title: Landing Page Optimization: Above the Fold, Trust, and Forms
lessons.title_es: Optimización de páginas de destino: por encima del pliegue, confianza y formularios
lessons.description: Apply the specific landing page elements that consistently move conversion rates.
lessons.description_es: Aplica los elementos específicos de páginas de destino que consistentemente mejoran las tasas de conversión.
lessons.content:
The 5-Second Test

A landing page visitor decides within 5 seconds whether to stay or leave. In those 5 seconds, they answer one question: Is this relevant to what I was looking for? The above-the-fold section must answer: What is this? Who is it for? Why should I care? What do I do next? If any of these four questions is unclear above the fold, you're losing visitors before they read a word of your copy.

Above-the-Fold Framework

The proven above-the-fold structure for service business landing pages:

  • Headline: Outcome-focused, specific.

Test 3–5 headline angles: outcome, problem, method, social proof, curiosity.
  • Subheadline: Expands on the headline. Addresses the

question.
  • Hero visual: Photo of real results, real clients, or the product in use. Avoid stock photos. A/B test: photo of a person (looking at camera) vs. product screenshot vs. before/after graphic.
  • Primary CTA: One button, high contrast, specific text.

for lead gen. Make the CTA visible without scrolling on mobile.
  • Social proof element: Logo bar of clients OR a single strong testimonial with a photo and full name. Place it immediately below the hero, not at the bottom of the page.

    • Trust Architecture

    Visitors who don't trust you don't convert. Trust elements and their conversion impact:

    • Real testimonials with photos and full names: +20–40% conversion. Never use fake stock-photo testimonials.
    • Specific case study result:

removes the conversion barrier of perceived risk.
  • Security badges and trust seals: Important for payment pages, minimal impact on lead gen pages.

    • Form Optimization

    Every field you add to a form reduces completion rate by 11% on average. Audit every field: is this information required before the first conversation? Phone number optional vs. required can swing completion rate by 30%. For lead gen, the optimal form is Name + Email + One qualifying question. Collect everything else after the first touchpoint.

    Multi-step forms convert 20–30% better than single-page forms for complex lead gen. Reason: commitment and consistency — once a user has filled step 1 (low effort), they're invested in completing steps 2 and 3.

lessons.title: A/B Testing Methodology: Design, Statistics, and Decision-Making
lessons.title_es: Metodología de pruebas A/B: diseño, estadística y toma de decisiones
lessons.description: Run A/B tests that produce reliable, actionable results — not false positives.
lessons.description_es: Ejecuta pruebas A/B que produzcan resultados confiables y accionables, no falsos positivos.
lessons.content:
Why Most A/B Tests Are Wrong

The majority of A/B test

reported by marketing teams are statistical noise. Common failure modes: peeking (checking results before reaching statistical significance and stopping early), multiple testing (running 10 tests simultaneously and cherry-picking the winner), underpowered tests (too few visitors to detect a real difference), and novelty effect (a change wins because it's new, not because it's better — effect disappears after 2 weeks). Understanding these failures is more important than knowing which tool to use.

Sample Size Calculation

Calculate required sample size before launching any test. Inputs: baseline conversion rate (your current CVR), minimum detectable effect (the smallest improvement worth detecting — typically 10–20% relative), statistical power (80% is standard), significance level (95% is standard, p < 0.05). Use a sample size calculator (Evan Miller's or AB Testguide). For a page converting at 3%, detecting a 20% improvement (to 3.6%) requires ~2,000 visitors per variant. Do the math before you start — don't run a test with insufficient traffic.

Test Design Rules

  • One variable at a time: Changing headline AND button color in the same test means you can't attribute the result. Exception: multivariate testing with a full factorial design and sufficient traffic (rare in practice).
  • Run for full weeks: Day-of-week effects are real. Run for at least 2 full weeks regardless of when you hit sample size — weekly traffic patterns must be balanced.
  • Test on primary traffic source: If 70% of your traffic is organic search, test on organic visitors. Don't pool all sources — different audiences respond differently.
  • Pre-register your hypothesis: Write down what you expect to happen and why, before seeing any results. This prevents retrofitting explanations to outcomes.

Reading Results and Making Decisions

When a test reaches statistical significance: if the variant wins by your MDE or more, implement it. If the result is below MDE, call it a draw — the effect is too small to matter. If the variant loses, document the hypothesis and the failure reason — a documented failure is a learning asset.

Maintain a Test Log: hypothesis, variable tested, traffic allocation, duration, results, and winner. After 20+ tests, mine the log for patterns. What types of changes consistently win? What types consistently fail? This meta-learning is more valuable than any individual test result.

lessons.title: Checkout Optimization, Cart Recovery, and Post-Purchase CRO
lessons.title_es: Optimización del checkout, recuperación de carritos y CRO post-compra
lessons.description: Recover lost revenue at the final conversion stages with proven checkout and post-purchase tactics.
lessons.description_es: Recupera ingresos perdidos en las etapas finales de conversión con tácticas probadas de checkout y post-compra.
lessons.content:
The Checkout Abandonment Problem

Average checkout abandonment rate across ecommerce is 70%. For every 10 customers who start checkout, 7 leave without buying. The revenue recovery opportunity is massive. The root causes of abandonment cluster into five categories: unexpected costs (shipping, taxes), forced account creation, trust concerns, payment friction, and distraction. Your checkout optimization roadmap should address these in order.

Checkout Page Optimization

Guest checkout: Mandatory. Forced account creation kills 30% of checkouts. Add account creation as an optional step on the confirmation page —

— after payment is captured.

Progress indicator: Show users where they are (Step 1 of 3: Shipping). Reduces abandonment by giving a sense of near-completion.

Trust elements at checkout: Security badge, accepted payment logos, and a 1–2 line guarantee statement immediately adjacent to the payment form. Not at the bottom of the page — next to the

button.

Address autocomplete: Google Places API integration on address fields. Reduces checkout time by 40% and eliminates address entry errors that cause fulfillment failures.

One-click upsell: After checkout completes, present a single complementary offer with a one-click add button. Post-purchase upsells convert at 15–25% because payment resistance is gone.

Cart Recovery Email Sequence

Three-email cart recovery sequence with timing and conversion benchmarks:

  • Email 1 — 1 hour after abandonment:

Product image, price, direct checkout link. No discount. Conversion rate: 5–8%.
  • Email 2 — 24 hours: Add a testimonial or social proof element. Subtle urgency (

if true). Still no discount. Conversion rate: 3–5%.
  • Email 3 — 72 hours: Offer a small incentive (5–10% off, free shipping) for fence-sitters. Conversion rate: 2–4%.

    • Together, a well-executed 3-email sequence recovers 8–15% of abandoned carts.

    Post-Purchase CRO

    CRO doesn't stop at the first conversion. Post-purchase optimization increases customer lifetime value without additional acquisition spend. Three high-impact post-purchase tactics: immediate cross-sell (one-click upsell on the confirmation page), review request sequence (email at day 7 and day 30 requesting a review — reviews improve future conversion rates by 15–25%), and loyalty trigger (after second purchase, invite to referral program). Measure post-purchase CVR as a separate KPI from first-purchase CVR.
